EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme winter storm affected all of southeast Idaho the first 4 days of the month.   Extreme amounts of Pacific moisture combined with several short waves and a cold frontal passage brought widespread snow amounts over a foot.  With temperatures near freezing, widespread damage occurred due to the heavy wet nature of the snow especially in the Snake River Plain.   Although there was a break in the snow late on the 2nd, it returned on the 3rd and continued into the 4th.   Strong winds were also a common feature with the multi day storm with widespread gusts over 40 mph causing blowing and drifting snow and low visibility by the 3rd and 4th when colder temperatures arrived.  Road closures were widespread.   Thunderstorms occurred with the cold frontal passage early on the 2nd.   School closures also occurred on the 4th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy snow fell in the Caribou Range and combined with strong winds to cause power outages and road closures on the 2nd with very wet snow.   Road closures also occurred on the 4th with strong winds and blowing and drifting snow.    The Swan Valley COOP observer recorded 27 inches of snow.   US Highway 26 closed from Ririe to Swan Valley on the 2nd and also on the 4th.   Lower Valley Energy reported power outages from Palisades Reservoir to Swan Valley during the morning of the 2nd.   Idaho Highway 31 closed on the 4th from Swan Valley to Victor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough brought several frontal passages which brought several days of blizzard like conditions and widespread heavy snow. Thunderstorms and snow squalls with heavy winds caused power outages in Mountain Home, Idaho. Schools were closed across the Treasure Valley area due to travel difficulties from heavy snow. Highway 20 in the Camas Prairie was closed for several days due to blizzard like conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported for nearly 33 customers near Mountain Home due to wind gusts bringing power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Arctic Front brought very frigid air behind it for January 11 through 16. The Panhandle had several days of single digit highs and negative teen lows. It caused damage to several buildings due to pipes bursting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme cold temperatures of highs in the single digits and lows in the negative teens caused several locations through the Coeur D'Alene area to have pipes burst or freeze. This closed at least 5 local businesses and schools including Bovill Elementary, North Idaho Stem, and Fernan Stem Academy. Post Falls Library was also damaged from freezing pipe burst. Damage cost is estimated with most being from Posts Falls Library.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frozen pipe burst in Athol Public Library caused damage to facility. Temperatures in the area ranged from from single digits for highs and negative mid teens for lows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For January 17 through 19, a Low brought another round of widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle 6 to 12 inches. It was followed by a period of freezing rain on the January 20 and 21. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to two tenths of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 8 inches snow 4 miles South of State Line. Other observations in the area ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avalanche near Stevens Peak stranded 3 people, injured one and killed one. The area had received 29.7 inches of snow since January 3.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall affected the Lewiston and Clarkston areas between 545 and 630 pm. Webcams indicated near-whiteout conditions. Snow rates exceeding one inch per hour were reported. KLWS reported quarter-mile visibilities for over 30 minutes. Law enforcement reported several roads closed in Lewiston. Time based on ASOS data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind causing low visibility caused a multi-vehicle accident on US Highway 30 north of Grace at 240 PM MST. It caused the highway to be closed between Lava Hot Springs and Soda Springs.  50 to 60 mph wind gusts were widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme winter storm affected all of southeast Idaho the first 4 days of the month.   Extreme amounts of Pacific moisture combined with several short waves and a cold frontal passage brought widespread snow amounts over a foot.  With temperatures near freezing, widespread damage occurred due to the heavy wet nature of the snow especially in the Snake River Plain.   Although there was a break in the snow late on the 2nd, it returned on the 3rd and continued into the 4th.   Strong winds were also a common feature with the multi day storm with widespread gusts over 40 mph causing blowing and drifting snow and low visibility by the 3rd and 4th when colder temperatures arrived.  Road closures were widespread.   Thunderstorms occurred with the cold frontal passage early on the 2nd.   School closures also occurred on the 4th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very powerful storm brought a combination of heavy wet snow and strong winds to cause extensive damage the first 4 days of March.  Snow amounts ranged from 10 to 18 inches with the majority falling on the 1st and 2nd.   A cold front early on the 2nd brought 40 to 60 mph wind gusts and combined with the heavy snow to cause extensive tree damage with many trees downed.   Power outages reported by Idaho Power and Rocky Mountain Power numbered over 50 thousand customers from Firth to Blackfoot to American Falls to Pocatello.  Several reports of downed power lines in Blackfoot, and Pocatello.   Fifteen power poles snapped off along US Highway 91 between Blackfoot and Fort Hall.  Several accidents on interstate 15 on the 2nd and interstate 86 as well along the interstate 15 south bound ramp with several vehicles damaged.   Snow and wind continued on the 3rd and 4th as temperatures were much colder and blowing and drifting snow across roadways were a bigger problem.   Due to snow and wind the following schools closed on the 4th:  Snake River District 52, Blackfoot District 55, Firth District 59, Aberdeen District 58, Pocatello/Chubbuck District 25, American Falls District 381, and Idaho State University.    The two day precipitation total of 2.01 inches on the 1st and 2nd at the Pocatello Airport was a two day record for the city.  11.6 inches of snow was measured as well the 1st through the 3rd.   11 inches fell in downtown Pocatello with up to 14 to 16 inches on the benches.   American Falls COOP reported 11.8 inches and Blackfoot reported 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved through the area. While most areas received amounts of up to 3 inches. There were some localized higher amounts of five to eight inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer estimated 8 inches of snow 9 miles Southeast of Elmira.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, March 9th, at approximately 3:00pm a snowmobiler unintentionally triggered a hard slab avalanche while riding up an open, east-facing, wind-loaded slope. The avalanche started at 6,700��� feet just below Gunsight Peak, in the headwaters of McCormick Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avalanche near Gunsight Peak resulted in 1 fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme winter storm affected all of southeast Idaho the first 4 days of the month.   Extreme amounts of Pacific moisture combined with several short waves and a cold frontal passage brought widespread snow amounts over a foot.  With temperatures near freezing, widespread damage occurred due to the heavy wet nature of the snow especially in the Snake River Plain.   Although there was a break in the snow late on the 2nd, it returned on the 3rd and continued into the 4th.   Strong winds were also a common feature with the multi day storm with widespread gusts over 40 mph causing blowing and drifting snow and low visibility by the 3rd and 4th when colder temperatures arrived.  Road closures were widespread.   Thunderstorms occurred with the cold frontal passage early on the 2nd.   School closures also occurred on the 4th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought heavy snow, strong winds which caused excessive damage due to the heavy and wet nature of the snow.   Several trees were downed with extensive power outages.   Windy conditions and colder temperatures caused some road closures on the 4th.  15 thousand customers from Rigby to Rexburg to Idaho Falls to Lewisville were without power on the 2nd.  10 to 16 inches of snow fell with 12 inches in Idaho Falls with 15 inches near the Idaho Falls Airport and 10 inches in Rexburg.   The Rigby COOP reported 9 inches.  Extensive reports of downed power lines.    A power pole snapped in Idaho Falls.   Dozens of trees downed in Idaho Falls.  A large carport collapsed on top of 14 vehicles at Westwood Park Village on Vega St in Idaho Falls.  A storage building collapsed as well at S yellowstone Highway and W 65th S south of Idaho Falls.  Power lines down throughout Lewisville as well.  A vehicle rollover along US Highway 20 on the morning of the 2nd between Idaho Falls and Sugar City.  Most of the property damage occurred due to the heavy snow on the 1st and 2nd.  By the 4th wind and blowing snow closes E Sunnyside Road in Hawks landing area and east in Idaho Falls.   US Highway 26 closed as well on the 4th from Ririe to Swan Valley due to blowing and drifting snow . Bone road closed in Iona.  Idaho Highway 33 closed from Newdale to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low passed through the Pacific Northwest over January 6 through 10. It brought several rounds of locally heavy snow and gusty winds. Through the period, locations in the North Idaho Panhandle saw snow amounts of 1 to 2 feet. The Lower Idaho Panhandle had amounts of 4 to 10 inches. Wind gusts ranged from 40 to low 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 6 inches of snow 13.9 miles Northeast of Sandpoint.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme winter storm affected all of southeast Idaho the first 4 days of the month.   Extreme amounts of Pacific moisture combined with several short waves and a cold frontal passage brought widespread snow amounts over a foot.  With temperatures near freezing, widespread damage occurred due to the heavy wet nature of the snow especially in the Snake River Plain.   Although there was a break in the snow late on the 2nd, it returned on the 3rd and continued into the 4th.   Strong winds were also a common feature with the multi day storm with widespread gusts over 40 mph causing blowing and drifting snow and low visibility by the 3rd and 4th when colder temperatures arrived.  Road closures were widespread.   Thunderstorms occurred with the cold frontal passage early on the 2nd.   School closures also occurred on the 4th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell and combined with strong winds to bring extensive travel problems with periods of road closures for 4 days.   15 inches of snow was measured in Driggs.   Idaho Highway 32 was closed from Ashton to Tetonia on the 1st and 2nd.  Idaho Highway 33 was closed on the 2nd from Newdale to Tetonia.  Driggs and the Teton Valley had extensive power outages on the 2nd.   US Highway 20 was closed from Ashton to the Montana border on the 2nd.  Idaho Highway 32 closed from Ashton to Tetonia was closed on the 4th.  Idaho Highway 47 was closed from Ashton to Bear Gulch on the 4th.  Idaho Highway 33 was closed from Newdale to Tetonia on the 4th.  The closure caused by a 15 vehicle pileup in the river Rim area at around 2 PM on the 4th with extensive blowing and drifring snow and low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho brought widespread damage to Idaho County. Around 3,000 lightning strikes occurred from Idaho to North Dakota which contributed to new wildfire starts in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force winds caused hundreds of trees, of all types, and diameters up to 2 feet wide, to be either uprooted or snapped in half along the track of the derecho from between Grangeville and Pollock, east and north towards Lolo Pass. Much of the Lochsa Corridor east of Kooskia sustained massive wind damage, and blocked access to many main and side roads. Multiple people were trapped for hours until people came to cut up the trees with chainsaws. Around 58 trees had to be cut along Forest Service road 221 southeast of Grangeville. A large willow tree fell on a house in Pollock and totaled it. A woman was injured from falling trees near Moose Creek where trees snapped off 20 feet above the ground and some damaged the ranger station there. Multiple vehicles, campers and infrastructure were either damaged or totaled at the Powell, Wendover, and Whitehouse campgrounds. The forest service estimates that 1 out of every 6 leave trees were uprooted in portions of their forest. Other impacts include: widespread power outages that lasted for days, multiple power poles damaged, at least 69 lightning-caused wildfires, 911 call center was inundated with 100s of calls, a motorcyclist was run off the road on U.S. Highway 12, and rock slides between mileposts 128 and 133 on U.S. 12. One of the lightning strikes triggered the Wye fire which turned into a large, 11,000 acre Moose Creek Complex. The emergency manager of Idaho County mentioned that some areas saw some of the worst damage they had ever experienced.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two heatwaves that occurred in July of 2024. The first heatwave occurred during the second week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were observed in both the Snake River and Salmon River valleys. The Pittsburg Landing RAWS saw its highest reading of 111 degrees on the 9th and 10th. Its low temperatures were in the low to mid 70s with a 76 low on the morning of the 11th. The Riggins COOP station saw its highest temperature of 104 degrees on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough passage coincided with deep moisture and instability to create strong afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A witness described multiple living, mature Douglas Fir and Grand Fir trees snapped in half. After reviewing the radar data, it appears a microburst occurred. After the thunderstorm pulsed up over the headwaters of the American River basin, north of Elk City, it then dropped over the Selway River between Selway Falls and Glover Campground. For these healthy trees to be snapped in half, the wind speeds could have exceeded 70 mph. It was reported that some of the trees were positioned towards the west-southwest direction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Salmon River Road as brief but intense rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and mudslides occurred along Salmon River Road from just above Ebenezer Campground to just below Owl Creek. Radar estimates of up to 1.25 inches of rain fell over a half hour period across the southern half of the Owl Creek Basin. There were three major slides along the Salmon River Road, near Poverty Flat, Skull Gulch, and one roughly 0.3 miles in length where the road bank collapsed into the river. The Skull Gulch slide destroyed at least one outbuilding. There were also 30 to 50 additional small slides across the Salmon River Road that didn't cause structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Bench Lake Fire began on July 11th and continued through the month eventually becoming 85 percent contained by the end of the month.  It was located in Custer County near Redfish Lake and it closed the Redfish Lake Recreation Complex for a period of time.  Closed trails were Alpine Way Trail, between Marshal Lake and Redfish Lake Lodge, Fishhook Trail 186, Redfish Trail to Baron Creek 101 and all trails heading into the Bench Lakes.   The fire grew to 2,595 acres with over three hundred personnel working on it at one point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The human caused Bench Lake Fire began on July 11th and continued through the month eventually becoming 85 percent contained by the end of the month.  It was located in Custer County near Redfish Lake and it closed the Redfish Lake Recreation Complex for a period of time.  Closed trails were Alpine Way Trail, between Marshal Lake and Redfish Lake Lodge, Fishhook Trail 186, Redfish Trail to Baron Creek 101 and all trails heading into the Bench Lakes.   The fire grew to 2,595 acres with over three hundred personnel working on it at one point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability, along with weak steering flow resulted in slow moving thunderstorms containing heavy rain during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced 1 to 2 inches of rain over 3 hours on Highway 162 just southwest of Kamiah. Flash flooding occurred along Fort Misery Road just south of Highway 162 with 1.5 to 2 miles of roadway destroyed. Debris from Fort Misery Road washed on to Highway 162 with the highway closed around 12 hours. One car was caught in the debris flow but no injuries were reported. Also, one power pole was lost in the debris slide causing 8 customers to lose power. Seven Mile Creek came out of its banks, and began forming a new channel just upstream from its confluence with Lawyer Creek. Water and some debris also washed over Highway 12 in several places between mile markers 63 to 66 (mostly Lewis County) requiring brief mitigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed along a surface convergence zone in the afternoon of July 25th.  One of the the storms near Glenns Ferry, Idaho spun up a brief surface based circulation into a brief land spout tornado which produced damage in Glenns Ferry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media and social media provided videos show a weak EF-0 land spout tornado on the ground in Glenns Ferry, with a path along and just north of E. Harrison Ave. Elmore County Emergency Management and general public reports, along with photos of roof damage, several downed trees, downed  branches, and damage to power lines are consistent with EF-0 wind speeds of 70 to 80 mph. The tornado had a path length of approximately 0.3 miles and a path width of approximately 30 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Vanity Wildfire began September 3rd from lightning strikes about 42 miles west of Challis and burned 600 acres near the Halstead Fire of 2012 site.  Forest Service Road #40008 Beaver Creek Rd from Vanity Summit to Josephus Lake was closed due to the fire . About 25 fire fighters were assigned to the fire.  Subalpine fir was the main fuel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vanity Wildfire began September 3rd from lightning strikes about 42 miles west of Challis and burned 600 acres near the Halstead Fire of 2012 site. Forest Service Road #40008 Beaver Creek Rd from Vanity Summit to Josephus Lake was closed due to the fire . About 25 fire fighters were assigned to the fire. Subalpine fir was the main fuel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wapiti fire began July 24th from lightning strikes near Grandjean in the Boise National Forest for eventually spread into the Sawtooth and Salmon Challis National Forests in the latter part of August and continued burning through September in the Sawthooths and Salmon Challis.  Idaho State Highway 21 was closed from Stanley to Lowman in late August and remain closed unto September 18th.  The Idaho Transportation Board approved 2 million dollars of highway repair for Highway 21 due to several miles of guardrail replacement as well as sign replacement and damaged pavement.  The fire burned over 129,000 acres.   Stanley schools were closed the first 7 days of classes in the 2024 school year due to extreme air quality issues from wildfire smoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wapiti fire began July 24th from lightning strikes near Grandjean in the Boise National Forest for eventually spread into the Sawtooth and Salmon Challis National Forests in the latter part of August and continued burning through September in the Sawthooths and Salmon Challis. Idaho State Highway 21 was closed from Stanley to Lowman in late August and remain closed unto September 18th. The Idaho Transportation Board approved 2 million dollars of highway repair for Highway 21 due to several miles of guardrail replacement as well as sign replacement and damaged pavement. The fire burned over 129,000 acres. Stanley schools were closed the first 7 days of classes in the 2024 school year due to extreme air quality issues from wildfire smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a closed low pressure system scattered thunderstorms developed from Lemhi County into Southwest Montana. Storms produced brief heavy rain and strong outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong cold front triggered thunderstorms and caused the temperature to drop from 59 degrees to 46 in an hour. Wind gust occurred with thunderstorm outflow. No storm damage noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Dust Storm from the Washington Columbia Basin impacted extreme western edge of the Low Idaho Panhandle. It was driven by the passage of a dry cold front during the afternoon of September 29. Wind gusts ranged from 35 to 42 MPH in the area. The impact was reduced visibility to less a quarter of a mile along Highway 95 near Tensed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported through social media that blowing dust reduced visibility less than a quarter mile on US 95 northbound 1 mile southeast of Tensed, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought a wintry mix to north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions were reported around the Camas Prairie and on Highway 14 to Elk City. There were numerous slide offs and disabled vehicles on Highway 95 from mile marker 257 to 261 which closed the highway for several hours the evening of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fire started on July 24 by lightning. It continued through August 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gwen Fire started on July 24 from lightning. It was contained on August 8. It burned 28820 acres of timber and grass. 156 structures were lost including 38 residences. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fire started on July 15 by an unknown human cause. It continued until August 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Texas Wildfire started July 15 from unknown human cause. It is located two miles East of Kendrick, Idaho. It continued until August 4. It burned 1582 acres of timber and 4 homes. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system with localized heavy snowfall caused extensive travel issues with one fatality possibly due to the road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall brought snow covered roads which led to numerous vehicle slide offs on interstate 15 between Pocatello and Idaho Falls.  Snowfall was light with most areas under one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system with localized heavy snowfall caused extensive travel issues with one fatality possibly due to the road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall and winds caused very hazardous roadways.  Only 1 to 3 inches of snow fell but US Highway 26 was closed from Ririe to Swan Valley due to blowing snow and reduced visibility.   As many as 27 vehicles reportedly slid off of interstate 15 between Pocatello and Idaho Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system with localized heavy snowfall caused extensive travel issues with one fatality possibly due to the road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pickup truck flipped over on interstate 15 west of Inkom at 1039 AM MST.  Total snowfall totals were 8.5 inches 6 miles north of Inkom, an estimated 13.6 inches at the Sedgwick Peak SNOTEL site and 9.2 inches at the Wildhorse Divide SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system with localized heavy snowfall caused extensive travel issues with one fatality possibly due to the road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal auto accident occurred at 216 PM MST on US Highway 26 at mile marker 391 near the north end of Palisades Reservoir.  A 66 year old male was killed and a 67 year old female was injured in what the Swan Valley Fire Department reported as very dangerous conditions.  US 26 was closed from 240 PM to 640 PM from Palisades Dam to the Wyoming border.   US Highway 26 also closed at 9 AM on the 15th due to blowing snow and reduced visibility.  Snowfall estimates at SNOTEL sites were 11 inches at Slug Creek Divide, 8 inches at Giveout, and 7 inches at Somsen Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region saw widespread snow with a strong moist system moving through the Pacific Northwest. It brought several inches of snow to portions of the Idaho Panhandle on December 18. It was followed by gusty winds near 55 MPH Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Magee Peak weather station reported a gust of 53 MPH. A reported 900 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry cold front pushed through the region on September 25. It generated several thunderstorms over Idaho Panhandle as it passed. The storms brought severe gusty outflow winds from downdrafts. The winds caused damage to trees and roofs. Recorded wind gusts ranged from up to 67 MPH. There was also a report of severe hail. A downed tree caused a fatality in Sandpoint. It brought dust storm to the Moscow area. Power outages reported by county: 1710 - Benewah, 18178 - Bonner, 3413 Boundary, 3328 -Shoshone, 8193 - Latah, 1019 - Kootenai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grain bin fell and landed on van. Pullman, Moscow Airport recorded a max gusts of 67 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of instability stretching over Lower Idaho Panhandle generated thunderstorms and heavy rain during the afternoon of September 11 2024. The storms caused a debris flow near the Gwen Fire burn scar and generated a funnel cloud over Mann Lake near Lapwai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported to local media an EF0 funnel cloud near Mann Lake at 1613 PDT. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of instability stretching over Lower Idaho Panhandle generated thunderstorms and heavy rain during the afternoon of September 11 2024. The storms caused a debris flow near the Gwen Fire burn scar and generated a funnel cloud over Mann Lake near Lapwai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report via social media stated rocks on Highway 12 within the Gwen Fire burn scar. This was confirmed by dispatch. Rocks were cleared and highway remained open. Nearby gauges reported 0.40 of an inch to 0.80 of an inch of rain over the last 18 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain  to north-central Idaho, impacting the Elkhorn Fire burn scar. These heavy rains lead to flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar and local mesonet observations indicate 0.75 to 1.00 inch of rain fell over 30 minutes across the Elkhorn burn scar. Large debris flows and scour paths occurred across the Salmon River Trail #096 for about 2.5 miles between Bargamin and Rattlesnake creeks. Multiple flow paths scoured at least 30 feet deep and 35 to 80 feet across where the trail crossed small draws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Victor measured over 11 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured 11.5 inches of snow in Victor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Panhandle had several days of near freezing temperatures. A strong low pressure with a decent plume of moisture during November 20 through November 21 time period brought decent snow amounts to North Idaho. Areas near the Canadian Border reported around 12 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer measured 11 inches of snow 2 miles North of Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry cold front pushed through the region on September 25. It generated several thunderstorms over Idaho Panhandle as it passed. The storms brought severe gusty outflow winds from downdrafts. The winds caused damage to trees and roofs. Recorded wind gusts ranged from up to 67 MPH. There was also a report of severe hail. A downed tree caused a fatality in Sandpoint. It brought dust storm to the Moscow area. Power outages reported by county: 1710 - Benewah, 18178 - Bonner, 3413 Boundary, 3328 -Shoshone, 8193 - Latah, 1019 - Kootenai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davis wx station recorded 57 mph gust. One large Pine down and some Aspen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the mountains of north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elk Butte SNOTEL received an estimated 8 inches of snow and 1.20 inches snow water equivalent.  Crater Meadows SNOTEL, located in the Clearwater Mountains in eastern Clearwater County at 5960 feet, received 7 inches of snow and recorded 1.10 inches of snow water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the mountains of north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass SNOTEL recorded 7 inches of snow and 0.60 inches of snow water equivalent.  Savage Pass SNOTEL recorded 5 inches of snow.
